51. Accounting for the Recent Decline in Global Income Inequality

American Journal of Sociology 2004-09-01 Available
JournalAmerican Journal of Sociology
Publication date2004-09-01
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1086/421541
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Glenn FirebaughPennsylvania State University; Institute of Criminology; Sociology and Criminology
Brian GoeslingUniversity of Michigan–Ann Arbor; University of Michigan; University of Michigan , Ann Arbor
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Following nearly two centuries of growth, global income inequality declined in the last decades of the 20th century. To determine the causes of that historic decline, we focus on income inequality across nations and find that the major equalizing force is faster‐than‐world‐average income growth in China and South Asia, industrializing regions where 40% of the world’s people live. Apparently what matters most about economic globalization thus far is its role in the spread of industrialization throughout populous poor regions of the world. If so, then globalization most likely has reduced global income inequality. This decline is anticipated to continue over the next few decades, first, because of the continued industrialization of poor regions and, second, because most of the growth in the world’s working‐age population will occur in poor regions.

KeywordsIncome, Poverty, and Inequality; Economic Theory and Policy; Economic Growth and Productivity; Industrialisation; Globalization; Economic inequality; Inequality; Development economics; Economics; China; Income distribution; Population; Demographic economics; Geography; Demography; Market economy; Sociology; Mathematical analysis; Archaeology; Mathematics
Matched conceptsAsia

52. The Characteristics of Chinese Personal Ties (Guanxi): Evidence from Hong Kong

Organization Studies 2004-09-01 Available
JournalOrganization Studies
Publication date2004-09-01
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1177/0170840604045092
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Irene Hau‐Siu ChowChinese University of Hong Kong; The Chinese University of Hong Kong, Hong Kong; The Chinese University of Hong Kong, Hong Kong#TAB#
Ignace NgUniversity of Victoria; University of Victoria, Canada
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

A widely held view in management is that guanxi(or Chinese personal connections) are a key element in the success of East Asian economies. In spite of their importance, very little is known about the various characteristics of guanxi. Using data obtained from Hong Kong respondents, we found that classmates, club members, and family friends are guanxibases from which the respondents developed close relationships. Contrary to expectations, associates who work with the respondents or are distant family relatives, or both, tend to have distant relationships with the respondents. We also found that the effect of gender on the closeness of relationships is asymmetrical, with female-to-female dyads being close and male-to-male dyads being distant. Further, our study shows that there is a tendency to request ‘costly’ favors from family members instead of nonfamily members. Lastly, we provide some evidence linking the size of one’s network to one’s ‘face’ in society. Overall, the results suggest a weakening of the family unit in Chinese societies.

KeywordsWork-Family Balance Challenges; Family Business Performance and Succession; Social Capital and Networks; Guanxi; Closeness; China; Face (sociological concept); Psychology; Social psychology; Unit (ring theory); Family ties; Strong ties; Interpersonal ties; Sociology; Political science; Genealogy; Law; History; Mathematics education; Mathematical analysis; Social science; Mathematics
Matched conceptsAsian

53. Politico–Economic Factors Associated with Interest in Starting a Business: A Multi–Country Study

Entrepreneurship Theory and Practice 2004-12-20 Available
JournalEntrepreneurship Theory and Practice
Publication date2004-12-20
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1111/j.1540-6520.2005.00068.x
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Thomas M. BegleyNortheastern University; Northeastern University and University College Dublin
Wee Liang TanSingapore Management University
Herbert SchochMacquarie University; Department of Accounting and Corporate Governance
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

In this article, we study the constructs of perceived environmental munificence and carrying capacity as they relate to potential for starting a business in samples taken from thirteen Anglo–Saxon, East Asian, and South Asian countries. Seven politico–economic dimensions represent perceived munificence and carrying capacity: financing available, supportive government regulation, market opportunities, access to support services, supply of skilled labor, connections needed, and competitive conditions. Perceived market opportunities, supply of skilled labor, and supportive government regulation (negatively) relate most consistently to start–up feasibility and desirability in the full sample. In regional subsamples, the only dimensions to associate with both feasibility and desirability are market opportunities in Anglo–Saxon countries and supply of skilled labor in South Asia.

KeywordsEntrepreneurship Studies and Influences; Corporate Finance and Governance; Family Business Performance and Succession; Government (linguistics); Sample (material); Business; Supply and demand; Market size; Economics; Labour economics; Commerce; Microeconomics; Philosophy; Linguistics; Chemistry; Chromatography

54. The Impact of Institutional Reforms on Characteristics and Survival of Foreign Subsidiaries in Emerging Economies*

Journal of Management Studies 2005-01-01 Available
JournalJournal of Management Studies
Publication date2005-01-01
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1111/j.1467-6486.2005.00488.x
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Chris Changwha ChungWestern University; University of Western Ontario
Paul W. BeamishWestern University; University of Western Ontario
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

abstract This study goes beyond the conventional notion of the institutional environment of emerging economies and investigates their dynamic context. It examines the complex influences of policy reforms on the characteristics and survival of foreign subsidiaries in emerging economies before and after the 1997 Asian Economic Crisis. This study proposes that FDI policy reforms during times of crisis may not only have a positive effect on institutional munificence for foreign firms, but such drastic reforms may also produce a negative effect (e.g. institutional volatility and complexity) on the environment. We consider how foreign firms effectively respond to the unfavourable forces of the post‐crisis environments while taking advantage of the munificence of the policy reforms. We find that foreign subsidiaries tend to take the form of wholly‐owned subsidiaries (versus joint ventures), majority joint‐ventures (versus minority joint‐ventures), or trading operations (versus manufacturing operations) in the post‐crisis institutional environment. Consistently, foreign subsidiaries with these characteristics are more likely to survive in the post‐crisis environment.

KeywordsCorporate Finance and Governance; International Business and FDI; Economic Policies and Impacts; Subsidiary; Emerging markets; Context (archaeology); Volatility (finance); Business; Foreign direct investment; Market economy; Economic system; International economics; Economics; Multinational corporation; Finance; Macroeconomics; Biology; Paleontology

55. A matrixed approach to designind it governance

MIT Sloan management review 2005-01-01 Available
JournalMIT Sloan management review
Publication date2005-01-01
DOI URLmissing
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Jeanne W. Rossnot available
Peter Weillnot available
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

On the basis of two different studies ? a survey of CIOs at 256 enterprises in the Americas, Europe and the Asia/Pacific region and a set of 40 interview-based case studies at large companies such as Johnson & Johnson, Carlson Companies, UPS, Delta Air Lines and ING DIRECT ? the authors conclude that when senior managers take the time to design, implement and communicate IT governance processes, companies get more value from IT. Toward that end, they offer a single-page framework for designing effective IT: a matrix that juxtaposes the five decision areas (principles, architecture, infrastructure, business-application needs, and prioritization and investment decisions) against six archetypal approaches (business monarchy, IT monarchy, federal, duopoly, feudal and anarchy).

56. Multiethnic Cities in North America

Annual Review of Sociology 2005-02-23 Available
JournalAnnual Review of Sociology
Publication date2005-02-23
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1146/annurev.soc.31.041304.122246
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Eric FongUniversity of Toronto; Department of Sociology, University of Toronto, Toronto, Ontario M5S 2J4, Canada;
Kumiko Shibuyanot available
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

The growing Hispanic and Asian populations in most major North American cities have drastically transformed the urban demographic landscape to become racially and ethnically diversified. We review literature on multiethnic cities by focusing on three important aspects of urban structures and processes: racial and ethnic residential patterns, ethnic businesses, and the performance of racial and ethnic groups in the labor market. Although the literature has identified many factors that shape these urban structures and processes, our discussion specifically focuses on the effects of multigroup contexts on urban structures and processes. We focus and compare four major racial and ethnic groups: whites, blacks, Hispanics, and Asians. Specific ethnic subgroups of all four groups are also discussed.

57. Competitive drivers and international plant configuration strategies: a product‐level test

Strategic Management Journal 2005-04-11 Available
JournalStrategic Management Journal
Publication date2005-04-11
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1002/smj.466
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Ren� BelderbosEindhoven University of Technology; KU Leuven; Katholieke Universiteit, Leuven, Belgium; and Technische Universiteit Eindhoven, Eindhoven, The Netherlands; Technische Universiteit Eindhoven, Eindhoven, The Netherlands; Katholieke Universiteit, Leuven, Belgium
Leo SleuwaegenVlerick Business School; Erasmus University Rotterdam; KU Leuven; Vlerick Leuven Gent Management School, Leuven, Belgium; Katholieke Universiteit, Leuven, Belgium; and Erasmus Universiteit, Rotterdam, The Netherlands; Katholieke Universiteit, Leuven, Belgium; Erasmus Universiteit, Rotterdam, The Netherlands; Vlerick Leuven Gent Management School, Leuven, Belgium
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Abstract We analyze the determinants of the decision to invest abroad and the choice of spatial configurations of overseas plants for 120 Japanese firms active in 36 well‐defined electronic product markets. We find that key competitive drivers at the firm and industry levels have a critical impact on the choice between alternative international plant configurations. Regional configurations focused on Asia are chosen by firms with weaker competitiveness for products with established manufacturing technologies. Plant configurations focused on the United States and the European Union are chosen by technology‐intensive firms facing competitive threats in foreign markets. Global configurations are chosen by firms with a strong competitive position in the Japanese and world market for their core product businesses and are more common in the case of strong oligopolistic rivalry between Japanese firms. Copyright © 2005 John Wiley &amp; Sons, Ltd.

58. Reversibility of the Other-Race Effect in Face Recognition During Childhood

Psychological Science 2005-06-01 Available
JournalPsychological Science
Publication date2005-06-01
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1111/j.0956-7976.2005.01554.x
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Sandy SangrigoliCentre National de la Recherche Scientifique; Laboratoire Vision Action Cognition; Laboratoire Cognition et Développement, CNRS, Boulogne-Billancourt, France
Christophe PallierInserm; Commissariat à l'Énergie Atomique et aux Énergies Alternatives; Institut d'Imagerie Biomédicale; INSERM U562, Service Hospitalier Frédéric Joliot, CEA/DSV/DRM, and IFR49, Orsay, France
A.-M. ArgentiCentre National de la Recherche Scientifique; Laboratoire de Sciences Cognitives et Psycholinguistique; École des hautes études en sciences sociales; Laboratoire de Sciences Cognitives et Psycholinguistique, ENS/CNRS/EHESS, Paris, France
Valérie A.G. VentureyraCentre National de la Recherche Scientifique; Laboratoire de Sciences Cognitives et Psycholinguistique; École des hautes études en sciences sociales; Laboratoire de Sciences Cognitives et Psycholinguistique, ENS/CNRS/EHESS, Paris, France
Scania de SchonenCentre National de la Recherche Scientifique; Hôpital Robert-Debré; Laboratoire Vision Action Cognition; Hôpital Robert Debré, Paris, France; Laboratoire Cognition et Développement, CNRS, Boulogne-Billancourt, France
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Early experience with faces of a given racial type facilitates visual recognition for this type of face relative to others. To assess whether this so-called other-race effect can be reversed by subsequent experience with new types of faces, we tested adults of Korean origin who were adopted by European Caucasian families when they were between the ages of 3 to 9. The adoptees performed a face recognition task with photographs of Caucasian and Asian faces. They performed exactly like a control group of French participants, identifying the Caucasian faces better than the Asiatic ones. In contrast, a control group of Koreans showed the reverse pattern. This result indicates that the face recognition system remains plastic enough during childhood to reverse the other-race effect.

KeywordsFace Recognition and Perception; Evolutionary Psychology and Human Behavior; Face recognition and analysis; Race (biology); Psychology; Facial recognition system; Face (sociological concept); Developmental psychology; Task (project management); Contrast (vision); Cognitive psychology; Artificial intelligence; Pattern recognition (psychology); Gender studies; Linguistics; Sociology; Management; Computer science; Economics; Philosophy
Matched conceptsAsian

59. Reflections on replication in OM research and this special issue

Journal of Operations Management 2005-12-15 Available
JournalJournal of Operations Management
Publication date2005-12-15
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1016/j.jom.2005.11.001
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Markham T. FrohlichBoston University; Boston University School of Management, 595 Commonwealth Avenue, Boston, MA 02251, USA
Jack DixonBoston University; Boston University School of Management, 595 Commonwealth Avenue, Boston, MA 02251, USA
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

“Replication allows us to demonstrate that something is really there, with extensions to see how broadly and in what instances it exists and can be used” (Hubbard et al., 1998). Most importantly, cumulative research is vital because theories are never proved or disproved (Hempel, 1966). Each successful verification simply provides greater support for a theory while every refutation raises concerns (Popper, 1978). Given that strong theories come from confirming and evolving ideas, replication sits at the very foundation of scientific knowledge (Flynn et al., 1990). Kuhn (1962) called this continual testing of theory “normal science”, and it accounts for a significant portion of the knowledge developed by a field inside a paradigm. In short—a literature dominated by un-replicated findings is of marginal value (Rosenthal and Rosnow, 1984). Every academic discipline needs a healthy balance between the publication of novel results and replicated studies. This special issue of the Journal of Operations Management exclusively features replication research. While replications are still relatively rare in the OM literature, we need to routinely assume more of these responsibilities. Towards that goal—the contribution of this special issue lies in not only longitudinally, methodologically, and globally testing existing ideas, but also in serving as encouragement to others interested in replicating research underlying OM models and theories. This special journal is the first entirely devoted to replication to appear in the OM field. As a result, it was a bit of an experiment with some interesting findings made in the process of compiling it. We would like to begin by thanking everybody who submitted a paper for this special issue, as well as the numerous reviewers for this unique batch of manuscripts. We would also like to give a special thanks to Rob Handfield – former Editor of the Journal of Operations Management – for his passionate belief in replication research and grand vision to help move the OM field forward by publishing more replications. In total, we had 25 submissions to the call-for-papers. This somewhat modest tally suggests that researchers may be reluctant to review other's work. Perhaps even more telling, without exception the papers were all replications of concepts first proposed a decade or more ago. Was this replication time lag out of respect to those authors or perhaps due to fear of ridicule or retaliation for testing more current ideas? Who knows, but given the difficulty of replicating studies, including mirroring data collection techniques and analyses, perhaps a 10-year lag is not unreasonable. Indeed, it may very well take a field a decade or more after an idea is first published for general consensus to be reached in terms of a study's overall importance, and by extension, its merits for replication. Having said that, we can’t help but contrast our field's replication track record with other academic disciplines. Take the famous case in Physics of “cold-fusion” beginning in 1989—perhaps one of the most disputed findings in the history of science. Physicists were quick to replicate the first experiments, as well as to explore the idea in a variety of new materials over the following years. Similarly, it is hard to imagine in medicine that important ideas will go unchallenged for decades. In the case of invasive medical treatments, preliminary findings are inevitably confirmed or refuted in due course by subsequent research as part of the overall march towards effective and safe treatments. A second finding was that many reviewers were not comfortable reviewing a replicated paper. It seems like many people want to read only the latest ideas, and as one prospective reviewer bluntly told us “I can’t waste my time looking at something that is already published”. Paradoxically, most readers that did review a replicated paper for this special issue found it interesting and enjoyable. To paraphrase one reviewer—it was kind of like reading a mystery-novel in that you could not wait to get to the paper's findings to see if the theory actually held up, and if so, what the extensions were. On the point of professional retaliation—in the course of putting together this journal we had the chance to informally talk to a variety of professors whose ideas were the subjects of replication. To a person—they were flattered that something that they had published years ago was studied anew. One author even mentioned that when they first published their findings they had wondered if it was truly a new phenomenon or perhaps just a “quirk in the data”. Over a decade later they were delighted (and relieved!) to hear that, “Yes” their earlier work had indeed broken new ground. Another observation concerns the stages of career of the researchers that submitted papers to this special issue. We, originally, thought that mostly new academics might take up the challenge of replicating concepts for this special issue—perhaps to “make a name” for themselves by shooting down some prominent theory. Surprisingly enough, papers for this special issue were submitted from all levels (Assistant, Associate, and Full Professors) in roughly the same ratios. Although a sample-size of 25 papers is hardly conclusive, we took this as good news in that OM researchers at all stages of their careers apparently see the merits of replication. Of course, our findings need to be replicated before we can draw any firm conclusions! A final comment is merited on the findings in all the papers submitted for this special issue. There are three possible outcomes from replication research as summarized in the flow chart shown in Fig. 1. Previous findings can be refuted, confirmed, or confirmed and extended. We argue that the two most desired outcomes are to either refute an existing concept or confirm and extend an idea. Simply confirming a concept, without “pushing it” a little further to test its generalizability or validity using different methods, seems to us to be a relatively marginal contribution and not suitable for publication in leading OM journals. Replication outcomes. When we first envisioned this call-for-papers, we suspected that a few papers might totally refute an existing theory or model. That did not occur, and in the end every single paper for this special issue, to varying degrees, affirmed previous work with important new extensions. Perhaps in the end, this is the true value of most replications; not to completely discredit another research team's work, but to add deeper insights, generalizability, and precision to previous findings so that a stream of research advances. The first paper in this special issue—Using Replication Research for Just-in-Time Purchasing Construct Development by Hale Kaynak and Janet Hartley—is a “classic” replication looking at the concept of Just-in-Time Purchasing (JITP) that was first introduced in the 1980s. Notably, this study used two different samples to test and extend existing JITP theory. The second paper—Revisiting the Theory of Production Competence: Extensions and Cross-Validations by Roger Schmenner and Gyula Vastag—takes another look at a widely cited OM conceptual model. While the replication supports the long-theorized links between operations and performance, it raises some important questions concerning previous findings that limited the overall framework to a relatively narrow set of contexts. The third paper—Information Systems Outsourcing; Replicating an Existing Framework in a Different Cultural Context by Subhashish Samaddar and Savitha Kadiyala—is an interesting cross-cultural replication that generalizes the findings from a Western information system outsourcing study to an Asian (i.e. Korean), context using case research methods. Not unexpectedly, the author found some conflicting evidence regarding outsourcing in different cultural settings. The fourth paper—Effects of Simplicity and Discipline on Operational Flexibility; An Empirical Reexamination of the Rigid Flexibility Model by Giovani da Silveira—replicated an existing model using an international data set. Equally noteworthy, this paper also tested the existing model across different process types in an effort to evaluate its overall robustness in variety of different operational contexts. The final paper—A Meta-Analysis of the Relationships Between Quality Management Practice and Firm Performance—Implications for Quality management Theory Development by Anand Nair—replicated existing findings using a method rarely employed in OM research—namely that of meta-analysis. This paper used data drawn across a range of previous studies published between 1995 and 2004 to test and refine the theorized relationships between quality management and performance. In generic terms, replications can be done along two dimensions. The first is in terms of using similar data and the second concerns methods. These replication options lead to the two-by-two shown in Fig. 2, which shows the four generic replication strategies. On one axis are data and the other methods. As summarized inside Fig. 2, this special issue features a paper in each of these possible quadrants, and by extension, these papers are in their own right models for a particular type of replication. Generic replication strategies. The answer to this question is virtually everything in our field. But are we, as a distinct academic discipline, up to the challenge? For the sake of argument—lets assume that of all the important OM ideas that have been published over the past 20 years very few have every been replicated. At the same time, there are almost certainly 3000–5000 active OM scholars around the world. That means that there are numerous OM academics per un-replicated idea, and if only a fraction of these scholars rise to the replication challenge, then we could dramatically strengthen our field's knowledge in the coming decade. Conversely, imagine if we let another 10 years go by without seriously challenging many of our most fundamental OM concepts. At that point we will end up with over a generation's worth of mostly one-off studies and unsubstantiated conclusions stretching from the early-1980s into the mid-2010s. If that comes to pass, who of us would be proud to look back upon what we as a field have left unaccomplished?

KeywordsQuality and Supply Management; ERP Systems Implementation and Impact; Innovation and Knowledge Management; Replication (statistics); Computer science; Psychology; Statistics; Mathematics
Matched conceptsAsian

60. Setting people up for success: How the Portman Ritz-Carlton hotel gets the best from its people

Human Resource Management 2006-01-01 Available
JournalHuman Resource Management
Publication date2006-01-01
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1002/hrm.20108
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Arthur YeungUniversity of Michigan–Ann Arbor; University of Michigan; (University of Michigan)
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

In China, where many multinational companies face a constant shortage of talent and high employee turnover, the Portman Ritz-Carlton Hotel has been able to attract, develop, and retain high-quality talent to deliver excellent customer service and ensure profitable growth. Under the leadership of Mark DeCocinis since 1998, the Portman Ritz-Carlton has not only been named as the “Best Employer in Asia” by Hewitt Associates three consecutive times, but has also rated the highest in employee satisfaction among all of the Ritz-Carlton's 59 hotels worldwide for five consecutive years. How can DeCocinis and his leadership team achieve such remarkable results? In his interview with Arthur Yeung, Mark DeCocinis, general manager of the Port-man Ritz-Carlton and regional vice president, Asia-Pacific, of the Ritz-Carlton Hotel Company, shares his philosophy and practices on talent management in China and elsewhere. © 2006 Wiley Periodicals, Inc.

KeywordsCruise Tourism Development and Management; Management; Multinational corporation; Economic shortage; Business; Business administration; Marketing; Economics; Finance; Philosophy; Government (linguistics); Linguistics

61. Venture Capital in Emerging Economies: Networks and Institutional Change

Entrepreneurship Theory and Practice 2006-03-01 Available
JournalEntrepreneurship Theory and Practice
Publication date2006-03-01
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1111/j.1540-6520.2006.00122.x
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
David AhlströmChinese University of Hong Kong; Chinese University of Hong Kong, Shatin, Hong Kong Special Administrative Region (SAR); The Chinese University of Hong Kong, Shatin, Hong Kong Special Administrative Region (SAR)
Garry D. BrutonTexas Christian University; M.J. Neeley School of Business at Texas Christian University, Fort Worth, Texas
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Emerging economies are characterized by fundamental and comprehensive institutional transformations as their economies begin to mature. How venture capitalist functions in environments that differ so fundamentally from those of the mature markets where venture capital was initially developed has only begun to be addressed. This article builds a framework to further the understanding of venture capital practice in emerging markets. Specific attention is focused on the impact of networks in the model, in particular, how networks and other informal institutions can act to supplement or replace formal institutions when they are weak. The article goes on to examine what the implications are for venture capital and the role of informal institutions in emerging economies as formal institutions become more established. The research and resulting model is grounded in 65 semistructured interviews with venture capitalists in emerging economies around East Asia. This article contributes to the literature by drawing attention to the impact of networks and changing institutional environments on venture capital during different phases of an economic transition process in emerging economies. The findings have implications for understanding institutional impact on venture capital activity as well as entrepreneurs and entrepreneurial ventures that seek venture capital financing in emerging economies.

KeywordsPrivate Equity and Venture Capital; Corporate Finance and Governance; Entrepreneurship Studies and Influences; Emerging markets; Venture capital; Social venture capital; Business; Market economy; Capital (architecture); New Ventures; Economics; Entrepreneurship; Finance; Economic system; Archaeology; History

62. Mixing Standard Work and Nonstandard Deals: The Consequences of Heterogeneity in Employment Arrangements

Academy of Management Journal 2006-04-01 Available
JournalAcademy of Management Journal
Publication date2006-04-01
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2006.20786085
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Joseph P. BroschakUniversity of Illinois Urbana-Champaign; University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign; University of Illinois at Urbana-ChampaignUniversity of Texas at Austin
Alison Davis‐BlakeUniversity of Illinois Urbana-Champaign; The University of Texas at Austin; University of Texas at Austin; University of Illinois at Urbana-ChampaignUniversity of Texas at Austin
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

We examined how proportions of individuals in standard and nonstandard work arrangements affected work group members ’ relationships with supervisors, social relations with coworkers, willingness to assist others, and intentions to leave their organization. Supporting Blalock’s theory of majority-minority group relations, higher proportions of nonstandard workers were associated with less favorable attitudes toward supervisors and peers, increased turnover intentions, and decreased work-related helping behaviors. The consequences of heterogeneity in employment arrange-ments were contingent on (1) workers ’ locations in their firm’s mobility system, (2) type of nonstandard arrangements, and (3) the amount and type of contact between stan-dard and nonstandard workers. The use of nonstandard work arrangements, such as temporary, contract, and part-time work (Kalle-berg, 2000; Kalleberg, Reskin, &amp;amp; Hudson, 2000) is becoming increasingly common. Kalleberg and his colleagues defined standard work as “work done on a fixed schedule—usually full-time—at the em-ployer’s place of business, under the employer’s control, and with the mutual expectation of contin-ued employment ” (Kalleberg et al., 2000: 258); non-standard work arrangements lack one or more of these attributes. The proportion of U.S. workers in nonstandard arrangements is estimated to be as high as 33 percent (Belous, 1989; Houseman &amp;amp; Po-livka, 2000), and the growth of new jobs being filled through nonstandard work arrangements is outpac-ing the growth of standard work arrangements (Be-fort, 2003). As of the end of the last decade, more than 90 percent of U.S. firms used some type of nonstandard workers (Von Hippel, Mangum, Greenberger, Heneman, &amp;amp; Skoglind, 1997), and the accelerating growth of nonstandard work arrange-ments is not limited to the United States. Non-standard work arrangements are also gaining popularity among firms in Canada, Europe, and parts of Asia (Allan, 2002; Connelly &amp;amp; Gallagher, 2004). Theorists and practitioners alike have claimed that blending nonstandard workers into work groups and organizations is essential to effective human resource management (Lepak &amp;amp; Snell, 1999;

KeywordsLabor Movements and Unions; Employment and Welfare Studies; Labor market dynamics and wage inequality; Turnover; Work (physics); Social psychology; Psychology; Demographic economics; Temporary work; Labour economics; Economics; Management; Engineering; Mechanical engineering

63. Beyond Guanxi: Network Contingencies in Taiwanese Business Groups

Organization Studies 2006-04-01 Available
JournalOrganization Studies
Publication date2006-04-01
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1177/0170840605058230
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Chi‐Nien ChungNational University of Singapore; National University of Singapore,
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Business groups consisting of legally independent affiliates with institutionalized relationships with each other exist in almost every market economy. While the groups are important and pervasive, little attention has been paid to the processes by which business groups emerge and grow. Reasons for this gap include a) a deficiency of the top-down, macro perspective of many business group studies, and b) the overemphasis of Guanxi networks in the literature of business organizations in East Asia. Personal relationships ( Guanxi) are treated as the necessary and sufficient condition for the entrepreneurship and evolution of business groups. In an attempt to refine the Guanxi perspective, the author uses case studies of five major Taiwanese business groups to propose a set of contingency factors. The suggestion offered in this paper is that entrepreneurs’ attributes and contextual factors are inseparable from the functions of the Guanxi network in the entrepreneurial process. Tracing the movements of these groups over the past four decades, it appears that group diversification evolves in a path-dependent fashion as opposed to a Guanxi-driven, idiosyncratic pattern as suggested by the Guanxi perspective. The influences of Guanxi for group diversification were clear in the early stages when markets were tightly controlled and the personalistic networks became the core capabilities underlining diversification. However, as groups grew and institutions developed, the significance of political Guanxi diminished and the decision-making of diversification strategy became hinged mainly upon the resources firm accumulated overtime.

KeywordsInnovation and Knowledge Management; Corporate Finance and Governance; Entrepreneurship Studies and Influences; Guanxi; Diversification (marketing strategy); Contingency; Business; Corporate group; Politics; Entrepreneurship; Perspective (graphical); Embeddedness; Economic geography; Sociology; Marketing; China; Economics; Political science; Law; Corporate governance; Social science; Epistemology; Artificial intelligence; Finance; Philosophy; Computer science

64. Policy‐Elite Perceptions and Social Movement Success: Understanding Variations in Group Inclusion in Affirmative Action

American Journal of Sociology 2006-05-01 Available
JournalAmerican Journal of Sociology
Publication date2006-05-01
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1086/499910
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
John D. SkrentnyUniversity of California, San Diego
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Using historical analysis of the inclusiveness of Labor Department affirmative action regulations for African‐Americans, American Indians, Asian Americans, Latinos, women, and white ethnics, this article shows that understanding variations in social movement success requires understanding policy‐elite perceptions of the meanings of social movements and the groups they represent. Variation in perceived meanings along dimensions of definition, morality, or threat helps explain the speed of group inclusion, the amount of mobilization needed, and possiblity for failure. Ethnoracial minorities benefited from perceptions of definitional and moral similarity to blacks, but elites perceived women as different definitionally and white ethnics as different definitionally and morally. Policy‐elite perceptions create obstacles for some groups, forcing them to struggle longer and harder for the same policy outcome.

KeywordsSocial and Cultural Dynamics; Gender Politics and Representation; Electoral Systems and Political Participation; Elite; Affirmative action; Inclusion (mineral); Perception; Morality; Social psychology; Action (physics); Collective action; Political science; Sociology; Social movement; Gender studies; Psychology; Law; Politics; Quantum mechanics; Neuroscience; Physics

65. Understanding Differences: Searching for the Social Processes that Construct and Reproduce Variety in Science and Economic Organization

Organization Studies 2006-08-01 Available
JournalOrganization Studies
Publication date2006-08-01
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1177/0170840606066309
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Richard WhitleyUniversity of Manchester; University of Manchester, UK; PMO Management and Organisation Studies
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Given the socially constructed nature of social phenomena, such as scientific knowledge and economic systems, and the considerable differences in how they are organized in different societies, the comparative analysis of variations in patterns of social organization and their consequences seems to me to be central to the social sciences. Beginning with the study of how and why scientific fields are differently organized and then comparing business schools and their graduates in Britain and France, a core theme in my intellectual career has been to understand how the organization of particular kinds of social activities varies between different contexts and has contrasting outcomes. More recently, this concern has been extended to the comparative analysis of economic organization in different institutional contexts, first of all in East Asia and then in Europe, leading to the elaboration of the comparative business systems framework. In all cases, I have developed intellectual frameworks for describing the central ways in which these phenomena varied and then attempted to show how particular kinds of institutional contexts can explain these differences. The same approach helps us to understand how the changing environment of research in business and management studies in many countries is influencing research goals and styles.

KeywordsEconomic Theory and Institutions; Management and Organizational Studies; Corporate Finance and Governance; Construct (python library); Variety (cybernetics); Theme (computing); Sociology; Social organization; Comparative research; Social science; Computer science; Operating system; Programming language; Artificial intelligence

66. Efficient Monte Carlo and Quasi–Monte Carlo Option Pricing Under the Variance Gamma Model

Management Science 2006-12-01 Available
JournalManagement Science
Publication date2006-12-01
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1287/mnsc.1060.0575
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Athanassios N. AvramidisUniversité de Montréal; Département d’Informatique et de Recherche Opérationnelle, Université de Montréal, C.P. 6128, Succ. Centre-Ville, Montréal, H3C 3J7, Canada
Pierre L’EcuyerUniversité de Montréal; Département d’Informatique et de Recherche Opérationnelle, Université de Montréal, C.P. 6128, Succ. Centre-Ville, Montréal, H3C 3J7, Canada
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

We develop and study efficient Monte Carlo algorithms for pricing path-dependent options with the variance gamma model. The key ingredient is difference-of-gamma bridge sampling, based on the representation of a variance gamma process as the difference of two increasing gamma processes. For typical payoffs, we obtain a pair of estimators (named low and high) with expectations that (1) are monotone along any such bridge sampler, and (2) contain the continuous-time price. These estimators provide pathwise bounds on unbiased estimators that would be more expensive (infinitely expensive in some situations) to compute. By using these bounds with extrapolation techniques, we obtain significant efficiency improvements by work reduction. We then combine the gamma bridge sampling with randomized quasi–Monte Carlo to reduce the variance and thus further improve the efficiency. We illustrate the large efficiency improvements on numerical examples for Asian, lookback, and barrier options.

KeywordsStochastic processes and financial applications; Mathematical Approximation and Integration; Statistical Methods and Inference; Monte Carlo method; Variance reduction; Estimator; Importance sampling; Control variates; Variance (accounting); Extrapolation; Quasi-Monte Carlo method; Computer science; Mathematical optimization; Monte Carlo integration; Mathematics; Hybrid Monte Carlo; Markov chain Monte Carlo; Statistics; Economics; Accounting

67. Diversification and performance: evidence from East Asian firms

Strategic Management Journal 2006-12-20 Available
JournalStrategic Management Journal
Publication date2006-12-20
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1002/smj.572
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Abhirup ChakrabartiDuke University; Fuqua School of Business, Duke University, Durham, North Carolina, U.S.A; Fuqua School of Business, Duke University; Durham North Carolina U.S.A
Kulwant SinghNational University of Singapore; Department of Business Policy, National University of Singapore, Singapore; [Department of Business Policy, National University of Singapore, Singapore]
Ishtiaq Pasha MahmoodNational University of Singapore; Department of Business Policy, National University of Singapore, Singapore; [Department of Business Policy, National University of Singapore, Singapore]
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Abstract We examine the impact of diversification on performance for firms operating in different institutional environments during a relatively stable period and during a major economy‐wide shock. We locate our study in six Asian countries at different levels of institutional development. Results indicate that diversification negatively impacts performance in more developed institutional environments while improving performance only in the least developed environments. Even in the least developed institutional environments, diversification offers limited benefits when an economy‐wide shock strikes. Though successful diversifiers are sometimes affiliated with business groups, diversification is associated with poorer performance for both affiliated firms and independent firms. In sum, we find that the outcomes of diversification are influenced by institutional environments, economic stability and affiliation with business groups. Copyright © 2007 John Wiley &amp; Sons, Ltd.

KeywordsCorporate Finance and Governance; International Business and FDI; Corruption and Economic Development; Diversification (marketing strategy); Shock (circulatory); Business; Economic geography; Economics; Industrial organization; Monetary economics; Marketing; Internal medicine; Medicine

68. Under and Beyond Constraints: Resource Allocation to Young Children from Biracial Families

American Journal of Sociology 2007-01-01 Available
JournalAmerican Journal of Sociology
Publication date2007-01-01
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1086/508793
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Simon ChengUniversity of Connecticut
Brian PowellIndiana University
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Using data from the Early Childhood Longitudinal Study, Kindergarten Class of 1998–99, the authors examine the extent to which biracial families differ from monoracial families in their transmission of resources to young children. In these analyses, the authors demonstrate the utility of distinguishing not only between white‐biracial and nonwhite‐biracial families and but also between even more refined measures of biracial families (e.g., white father/Asian mother). The authors find that, in most cases, biracial families provide comparable or greater economic and cultural resources to their children than do their monoracial counterparts, but offer fewer advantages in interactional/social resources. This overall pattern remains even after sociodemographic factors are taken into consideration. Exceptions to this pattern also are identified and explored. Implications for our understanding of racial stratification, interracial relations, and the role of both human agency and constraints on intergenerational transmission of resources are discussed.

KeywordsRacial and Ethnic Identity Research; Urban, Neighborhood, and Segregation Studies; School Choice and Performance; Agency (philosophy); Psychology; Developmental psychology; White (mutation); Social psychology; Demography; Sociology; Social science; Biochemistry; Gene; Chemistry

70. Examining Classified Boards

MIT Sloan management review 2007-01-01 Available
JournalMIT Sloan management review
Publication date2007-01-01
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-021-22153-9
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
David T. Wagnernot available
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Climate change affects precipitation patterns. Here, we investigate whether its signals are already detectable in reported river flood damages. We develop an empirical model to reconstruct observed damages and quantify the contributions of climate and socio-economic drivers to observed trends. We show that, on the level of nine world regions, trends in damages are dominated by increasing exposure and modulated by changes in vulnerability, while climate-induced trends are comparably small and mostly statistically insignificant, with the exception of South & Sub-Saharan Africa and Eastern Asia. However, when disaggregating the world regions into subregions based on river-basins with homogenous historical discharge trends, climate contributions to damages become statistically significant globally, in Asia and Latin America. In most regions, we find monotonous climate-induced damage trends but more years of observations would be needed to distinguish between the impacts of anthropogenic climate forcing and multidecadal oscillations.

KeywordsStructural Analysis of Composite Materials; Structural Engineering and Vibration Analysis; Business
Matched conceptsAsia

71. Social Boundaries and Marital Assimilation: Interpreting Trends in Racial and Ethnic Intermarriage

American Sociological Review 2007-02-01 Available
JournalAmerican Sociological Review
Publication date2007-02-01
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1177/000312240707200104
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Zhenchao QianThe Ohio State University; the Ohio State University
Daniel T. LichterCornell University
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Interracial/interethnic marriage in America is a barometer of racial/ethnic relations and intergroup social distance. Using data from the 5-percent Public Use Microdata Sample of the 1990 and 2000 censuses, we interpret trends in intermarriage in light of new assimilation theory, recent changes in racial classification, and rapid demographic changes in American society. Our results indicate that changes in marital assimilation have taken on momentum of their own; that is, America's growing biracial population has fueled the growth of interracial marriages with whites. Analyses also shed new light on the effects of rapid immigration, rising cohabitation, and educational upgrading on intermarriage patterns, and yield both continuities and departures from the past. Historic patterns of racial/ethnic differences in intermarriage persist—Hispanics and American Indians are most likely to marry whites, followed closely by Asian Americans. African Americans are least likely to marry whites. Yet, the 1990s brought significant increases in intermarriage between blacks and whites; large increases in cohabitation did not offset the growth of racially-mixed marriages. The past decade also ushered in unprecedented declines in intermarriage with whites and large increases in marriage between native- and foreign-born co-ethnics among Hispanics and Asian Americans. The role of educational attainment in the out-marriage patterns of Hispanics and Asian Americans was also reinforced. Any evidence of differential growth in African American-white marriages among the highly educated African American population was weak. If intermarriage is our guide, any shifting, blurring, or crossing of racial/ethnic boundaries represent uncommonly weak mechanisms for breaking down existing racial barriers to black-white union formation.

KeywordsMigration and Labor Dynamics; Urban, Neighborhood, and Segregation Studies; Racial and Ethnic Identity Research; Cohabitation; Ethnic group; Immigration; Microdata (statistics); Population; Demography; Educational attainment; Geography; Gender studies; Sociology; Census; Political science; Law; Archaeology; Anthropology

Abstract

This article examines the influence of citation impact on scholarship. Citation analysis allows authors to determine how often their work has been cited by others. This creates a quantitative hierarchy of influence and relevance, potentially boosting the author's reputation. However, the author suggests the pressure for prestige may distort the natural processes of research and publication. He analyzes citation impact through the perspective of East Asian management research and finds many researches are following Western models instead of offering innovative new theories.

73. Apparent Universality of Positive Implicit Self-Esteem

Psychological Science 2007-06-01 Available
JournalPsychological Science

DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1111/j.1467-9280.2007.01928.x
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Susumu YamaguchiThe University of Tokyo; University of Tokyo, Tokyo, Japan
Anthony G. GreenwaldUniversity of Washington
Mahzarin R. BanajiHarvard University Press; Harvard University
Fumio MurakamiThe University of Tokyo; University of Tokyo, Tokyo, Japan
Daniel ChenUniversity of Washington
Kimihiro ShiomuraShinshu University; Shinshu University, Nagano, Japan
Chihiro KobayashiOsaka University; Osaka University, Osaka, Japan
Huajian CaiUniversity of Washington
Anne C. KrendlMassachusetts Institute of Technology
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Trends in International Mathematics and Science Study found that even though children from all East Asian countries outperformed American children, American students reported higher self-evaluation of their math and science abilities than did students from East Asian countries such as China, Korea, and Japan (Mullis, Martin, Gonzalez, & Chrostowski, 2004). Such cross-cultural differences in self-appraisal fit the stereotype of the modest East Asian and contribute to the received view that East Asians have less positive self-concepts than Americans. This view was summarized recently by Heine, Lehman, Markus, and Kitayama (1999) as follows: The need for positive self-regard, as it is currently conceptualized, is not a universal, but rather is rooted in significant aspects of North American culture (p. 766; but cf. Sedikides, Gaertner, & Vevea, 2005).

KeywordsSocial and Intergroup Psychology; Cultural Differences and Values; Behavioral Health and Interventions; East Asia; Psychology; China; Stereotype (UML); Universality (dynamical systems); Asian culture; Stereotype threat; Social psychology; Self-esteem; Asian americans; Developmental psychology; Anthropology; Sociology; Geography; Archaeology; Quantum mechanics; Physics; Ethnic group
Matched conceptsAsia; Asian

74. Within-Person Changes in the Structure of Emotion

Psychological Science 2007-07-01 Available
JournalPsychological Science
Publication date2007-07-01
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1111/j.1467-9280.2007.01947.x
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
W. Q. Elaine PerunovicUniversity of Waterloo; University of Waterloo, Waterloo, Ontario, Canada; University of Waterloo, Waterloo, Ontario, CANADA
Daniel HellerUniversity of Waterloo; University of Waterloo, Waterloo, Ontario, Canada; University of Waterloo, Waterloo, Ontario, CANADA
Eshkol RafaeliColumbia University; Barnard College; Barnard College, Columbia University; *Barnard College, Columbia University
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

This study explored the within-person dynamic organization of emotion in East-Asian Canadian bicultural individuals as they function in two cultural worlds. Using a diary design, we examined under what conditions their emotional structure resembles that of Westerners or that of East Asians. As predicted, when these bicultural individuals identified with a Western culture or had recently spoken a non-Asian language, their positive and negative affect were inversely associated. When they identified with an Asian culture or interacted in an Asian language, this inverse association disappeared. This study shows that as bicultural individuals identify and communicate with members of one or the other cultural group, they may adopt a culturally congruent phenomenology, including a distinct affective pattern.

KeywordsCultural Differences and Values; Emotions and Moral Behavior; Language, Discourse, Communication Strategies; Psychology; Asian culture; Social psychology; Phenomenology (philosophy); Cultural diversity; Developmental psychology; Association (psychology); Affect (linguistics); Anthropology; Communication; Sociology; Epistemology; Philosophy; Psychotherapist
Matched conceptsAsian

75. Making career choice: A study of Chinese managers

Human Relations 2007-08-01 Available
JournalHuman Relations
Publication date2007-08-01
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1177/0018726707081661
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Amy Lai Yu WongHong Kong Polytechnic University; Hong Kong Polytechnic University,
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Compared with the extensive research on managerial careers in the West, little has emerged from China and South-East Asia. This exploratory study used Schein's concept of Career Anchors to investigate how 117 Taiwanese managers made their initial career choice and the values which shaped their subsequent careers. The findings indicate how cultural specificity and political dynamics impacted on the managers' career experience. The Taiwanese subjects valued independence, a balanced lifestyle and entrepreneurial opportunities. The findings further indicate how career pathways can be of an individual's own making, embracing life in its broadest aspects and not exclusively forged within organizations. The study sheds light on the field of vocational choice and adjustment within the Chinese context.

KeywordsEntrepreneurship Studies and Influences; Family Business Performance and Succession; Higher Education and Employability; China; Vocational education; Context (archaeology); Independence (probability theory); Exploratory research; Politics; Field (mathematics); Psychology; Career development; Sociology; Social psychology; Public relations; Political science; Pedagogy; Social science; Geography; Law; Pure mathematics; Mathematics; Archaeology; Statistics

76. Doing well by doing good—case study: ‘Fair &amp; Lovely’ whitening cream

Strategic Management Journal 2007-08-22 Available
JournalStrategic Management Journal
Publication date2007-08-22
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1002/smj.645
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Aneel KarnaniUniversity of Michigan; Ross School; Stephen M. Ross School of Business, University of Michigan, Ann Arbor, Michigan, U.S.A
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Abstract According to the ‘doing well by doing good’ proposition, firms have a corporate social responsibility to achieve some larger social goals, and can do so without a financial sacrifice. This research note empirically examines this proposition by studying in depth the case of ‘Fair &amp; Lovely,’ a skin whitening cream marketed by Unilever in many countries in Asia and Africa, and, in particular, India. Fair &amp; Lovely is indeed doing well; it is a profitable and fast‐growing brand. It is, however, not doing good, and I demonstrate its negative implications for public welfare. I conclude with thoughts on how to reconcile this divergence between private profits and public welfare. Copyright © 2007 John Wiley &amp; Sons, Ltd.

KeywordsCorporate Social Responsibility Reporting; Innovation and Socioeconomic Development; Proposition; Sacrifice; Social responsibility; Divergence (linguistics); Economics; Welfare; Fair trade; Social Welfare; Corporate social responsibility; Business; Law; Market economy; Political science; International trade; Philosophy; Archaeology; History; Epistemology; Linguistics

77. Cultural Differences in the Impact of Social Support on Psychological and Biological Stress Responses

Psychological Science 2007-08-23 Available
JournalPsychological Science
Publication date2007-08-23
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1111/j.1467-9280.2007.01987.x
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Shelley E. TaylorUniversity of California, Los Angeles; Department of Psychology, University of California, Los Angeles, CA 90095-1563, USA. [email removed]
William T. WelchUniversity of California, Los Angeles; University of California, Los Angeles/CA/US
Heejung S. KimUniversity of California, Santa Barbara; University of California, Santa Barbara, CA, US
David K. ShermanUniversity of California, Santa Barbara; University of California, Santa Barbara, CA, US
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Social support is believed to be a universally valuable resource for combating stress, yet Asians and Asian Americans report that social support is not helpful to them, resist seeking it, and are underrepresented among recipients of supportive services. We distinguish between explicit social support (seeking and using advice and emotional solace) and implicit social support (focusing on valued social groups) and show that Asians and Asian Americans are psychologically and biologically benefited more by implicit social support than by explicit social support; the reverse is true for European Americans. Our discussion focuses on cultural differences in the construal of relationships and their implications for social support and delivery of support services.

KeywordsCultural Differences and Values; Child and Adolescent Psychosocial and Emotional Development; Community Health and Development; Psychology; Social support; Social psychology; Construal level theory; Social stress; Emotional support; Developmental psychology
Matched conceptsAsian

78. The Soft Embodiment of Culture

Psychological Science 2007-08-23 Available
JournalPsychological Science
Publication date2007-08-23
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1111/j.1467-9280.2007.01986.x
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Angela K.‐Y. LeungSingapore Management University; Singapore Management University, Singapore
Dov CohenUniversity of Illinois Urbana-Champaign; University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Cultural assumptions about one's relation to others and one's place in the world can be literally embodied in the way one cognitively maps out one's position and motion in time and space. In three experiments, we examined the psychological perspective that Asian American and Euro-American participants embodied as they both comprehended and produced narratives and mapped out metaphors of time and space. In social situations, Euro-American participants were more likely to embody their own perspective and a sense of their own motion (rather than those of a friend), whereas Asian American participants were more likely to embody a friend's perspective and sense of motion (rather than their own). We discuss how these psychological perspectives represent the soft embodiment of culture by implicitly instantiating cultural injunctions (a) to think about how you look to others and to harmonize with them or (b) to know yourself, trust yourself, and act with confidence.

KeywordsCultural Industries and Urban Development; Soft power; Political science; Politics; Law
Matched conceptsAsian

Satoru ShimaTokyo Keizai University; Tokyo Keizai University, Tokyo, Japan
Alejandra Suarez SimoniUniversidad Privada de Santa Cruz de la Sierra; Private University of Santa Cruz of the Sierra, Bolivia
Oi Ling SiuLingnan University, Hong Kong,
Maria Widerszal‐BazylCentral Institute for Labour Protection; Central Institute for Labor Protection
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

A study of work interference with family (WIF) among managers is described, contrasting four clusters of countries, one of which is individualistic (Anglo) and three of which are collectivistic (Asia, East Europe, and Latin America). Country cluster (Anglo vs. each of the others) moderated the relation of work demands with strain‐based WIF, with the Anglo country cluster having the strongest relationships. Country cluster moderated some of the relationships of strain‐based WIF with both job satisfaction and turnover intentions, with Anglos showing the strongest relationships. Cluster differences in domestic help were ruled out as the possible explanation for these moderator results.

80. Environmental Shocks and SME Alliance Formation Intentions in an Emerging Economy: Evidence from the Asian Financial Crisis in Indonesia

Entrepreneurship Theory and Practice 2008-01-01 Available
JournalEntrepreneurship Theory and Practice
Publication date2008-01-01
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1111/j.1540-6520.2007.00220.x
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Louis MarinoHigh Institute of Management and Entrepreneurship; Entrepreneurship and strategic management in the Department of Management and Marketing and the Executive Director of the Alabama Entrepreneurship Institute at The University of Alabama
Franz T. LohrkeSamford University; Management, Entrepreneurship Program Coordinator, and Chair of the Management and Marketing Department at Samford University
John S. HillCivitan International; International Business at The University of Alabama
K. Mark WeaverHigh Institute of Management and Entrepreneurship; Entrepreneurship in the Rucks Department of Management and the Director of the Stephenson Entrepreneurship Institute at Louisiana State University
Tulus TambunanUniversitas Trisakti; Center of Industry and SME studies at Trisakti University, Jakarta, Indonesia
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Environmental shocks can occur when emerging country governments open their markets to outside influences. We extend research conducted primarily in mature economies on how managers react to environmental shocks by evaluating how environmental shock type, a firm's strategic orientation, and its slack resources affected strategic alliance formation intentions during and immediately following the Asian Financial Crisis. Results from two Indonesian small– and medium–sized enterprises (SME) samples show that these factors influenced alliance intentions, although not always in ways that were consistent with previous research findings in more mature markets. Overall, our results provide critical insights into emerging market firms’ strategic actions, particularly related to key managerial motivations for SME alliance formation.

KeywordsInternational Business and FDI; Corporate Finance and Governance; Family Business Performance and Succession; Alliance; Shock (circulatory); Business; Emerging markets; Indonesian; Financial crisis; Strategic alliance; Industrial organization; Market economy; Economics; Finance; Political science; Macroeconomics; Law; Medicine; Philosophy; Linguistics; Internal medicine

81. Challenging conventional wisdom about who quits: Revelations from corporate America.

Journal of Applied Psychology 2008-01-01 Available
JournalJournal of Applied Psychology
Publication date2008-01-01
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1037/0021-9010.93.1.1
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Peter W. HomArizona State University; Department of Management, Arizona State University, Temple, AZ 85287, USA. [email removed]; Department of Management, Arizona State University, Tempe, AZ, US
Loriann RobersonColumbia University; Teachers College,, Columbia University, New York, NY, US
Aimee Dars EllisArizona State University; Department of Management, Arizona State University, Tempe, AZ, US
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Findings from 20 corporations from the Attrition and Retention Consortium, which collects quit statistics about 475,458 professionals and managers, extended and disputed established findings about who quits. Multilevel analyses revealed that company tenure is curvilinearly related to turnover and that a job's past attrition rate strengthens the (negative) performance- exit relationship. Further, women quit more than men, while African Americans, Hispanic Americans, and Asian Americans quit more than White Americans, though racial differences disappeared after confounds were controlled for. African American, Hispanic American, and Asian American women quit more than men of the same ethnicities and White Americans, but statistical controls nullified evidence for dual discrimination toward minority women. Greater corporate flight among women and minorities during early employment nonetheless hampers progress toward a more diversified workforce in corporate America.

KeywordsGender Diversity and Inequality; Labor market dynamics and wage inequality; Work-Family Balance Challenges; Attrition; Psychology; Asian americans; Ethnic group; Workforce; White (mutation); Turnover; African american; Demography; Social psychology; Demographic economics; Management; Political science; Medicine; Sociology; Economics; Biochemistry; Gene; Ethnology; Dentistry; Chemistry; Law

82. Organizational and cultural entry: A new type of orientation program for multiple boundary crossings.

Journal of Applied Psychology 2008-01-01 Available
JournalJournal of Applied Psychology
Publication date2008-01-01
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1037/a0012828
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Jinyan FanHofstra University; Department of Psychology, Hofstra University, Hempstead, NY 11549, USA. [email removed]; Department of Psychology, Hofstra University, Hempstead, US
John P. WanousFisher College; Department of Management and Human Resources, Fisher College of Business, Ohio State University, Columbus, OH, US
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

A longitudinal field experiment was designed to test the efficacy of a new type of orientation program in facilitating sojourners' organizational and cultural entry. Focused on stress coping, this new approach to orientation has the following features: (a) a thorough needs assessment identified the major stressors from both organizational and cultural entry, (b) sojourners were provided with realistic information regarding their future tasks and environment, and (c) sojourners were taught various coping skills to handle the major stressors. Seventy-two new graduate students from Asia entering a large midwestern public university in the United States were randomly assigned to either a traditional orientation program or the new program. The participants were assessed immediately after the orientation, as well as at 1, 3, 6, and 9 months post-entry. Results show that participants in the new orientation program had lower pre-entry expectations, felt less stressed, and reported higher levels of academic and interaction adjustment at 6 and 9 months post-entry than participants in the traditional orientation program. Many of the beneficial effects from the new orientation experience were not immediately apparent; rather, they emerged over time. Sojourner stress was found to mediate some of the treatment effects, as predicted.

KeywordsInternational Student and Expatriate Challenges; Job Satisfaction and Organizational Behavior; Cultural Differences and Values; Psychology; Stressor; Entry Level; Coping (psychology); Social psychology; Longitudinal field; Orientation (vector space); Developmental psychology; Applied psychology; Clinical psychology; Medical education; Quantum mechanics; Magnetic field; Mathematics; Medicine; Geometry; Physics

83. How to Win in Emerging Markets

MIT Sloan management review 2008-01-01 Available
JournalMIT Sloan management review
Publication date2008-01-01
DOI URLmissing
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Satish Shankarnot available
Charles Ormistonnot available
Nicolas Blochnot available
Robert Schausnot available
Vijay Vishwanathnot available
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Emerging markets in Asia, Latin America and Eastern Europe are delivering some of the strongest revenue and profit growth for global makers of fast-moving consumer goods, despite concerns that lower prices might translate into lower profits. Emerging-market leaders like Coca-Cola, Unilever, Colgate-Palmolive, Groupe Danone and PepsiCo earn 5% to 15% of their total revenues from the three largest emerging markets in Asia ? China, India and Indonesia. The story is similar in Russia and Eastern Europe, where these companies often dominate their target categories and routinely exceed internal corporate benchmarks for profitability. And the trend is likely to continue. The gross domestic product of emerging markets equaled the GDP of advanced nations for the first time in 2006, with much of the growth coming from the ?BRICET? nations ? Brazil, Russia, India, China, Eastern Europe and Turkey.

84. Cultural Influences on Neural Substrates of Attentional Control

Psychological Science 2008-01-01 Available
JournalPsychological Science
Publication date2008-01-01
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1111/j.1467-9280.2008.02038.x
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Trey HeddenMassachusetts Institute of Technology; Stanford University
Sarah KetayState University of New York; Stony Brook University; State University of New York at Stony Brook
Arthur AronState University of New York; Stony Brook University; State University of New York at Stony Brook
Hazel Rose MarkusStanford University
John D. E. GabrieliMassachusetts Institute of Technology
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Behavioral research has shown that people from Western cultural contexts perform better on tasks emphasizing independent (absolute) dimensions than on tasks emphasizing interdependent (relative) dimensions, whereas the reverse is true for people from East Asian contexts. We assessed functional magnetic resonance imaging responses during performance of simple visuospatial tasks in which participants made absolute judgments (ignoring visual context) or relative judgments (taking visual context into account). In each group, activation in frontal and parietal brain regions known to be associated with attentional control was greater during culturally nonpreferred judgments than during culturally preferred judgments. Also, within each group, activation differences in these regions correlated strongly with scores on questionnaires measuring individual differences in culture-typical identity. Thus, the cultural background of an individual and the degree to which the individual endorses cultural values moderate activation in brain networks engaged during even simple visual and attentional tasks.

KeywordsCultural Differences and Values; Face Recognition and Perception; Neural and Behavioral Psychology Studies; Psychology; Context (archaeology); Functional magnetic resonance imaging; Cognitive psychology; Control (management); Social psychology; Neuroscience; Paleontology; Economics; Management; Biology
Matched conceptsAsian

85. Asian Management Research: Status Today and Future Outlook

Journal of Management Studies 2008-02-21 Available
JournalJournal of Management Studies
Publication date2008-02-21
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1111/j.1467-6486.2007.00758.x
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Garry D. BrutonTexas Christian University; Texas Christian University;
Chung‐Ming LauChinese University of Hong Kong
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

abstract This paper reviews the existing management research on Asia. The findings highlight that Asian management research not only builds the understanding of Asia, but also makes a critical contribution to the broad domain of management studies. In particular, Asian‐focused research allows researchers to extend and revise theories through the consideration of new contextual variables. This enables researchers to fine‐tune theories by developing context‐specific conditions and operationalization of key constructs, which in turn allows researchers to develop new theories and constructs which are generalizable to research in other contexts. This systematic examination will help to lay the foundation for the further expansion of the understanding of both Asia and of management research in general.

KeywordsGlobal and Cross-Cultural Management; Management and Organizational Studies; International Student and Expatriate Challenges; Operationalization; Context (archaeology); Management science; Foundation (evidence); Engineering ethics; Knowledge management; Sociology; Key (lock); Domain (mathematical analysis); Epistemology; Political science; Process management; Computer science; Business; Engineering; Geography; Computer security; Law; Mathematics; Archaeology; Philosophy; Mathematical analysis

86. Do race and gender matter in international assignments to/from Asia Pacific? An exploratory study of attitudes among Chinese

Human Resource Management 2008-02-27 Available
JournalHuman Resource Management
Publication date2008-02-27
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1002/hrm.20199
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Rosalie L. TungSimon Fraser University; Simon Fraser University (Canada)
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Abstract Based on a survey of EMBA students in China and South Korea, this article examines how two sensitive but potentially salient criteria—race and gender—affect the selection of an executive to head the (a) foreign operations of a U.S. multinational in China and South Korea and (b) newly acquired U.S. operations of a Korean multinational. The results reveal a fairly complex picture of how gender, race, and the interplay of these two factors might affect these decisions. In the Korean sample, competencies mattered more than race and gender in a senior executive appointment to the U.S. operations of Korean multinationals. Also in the Korean sample, race and gender outweighed competencies in assignments to Korea. In the Chinese sample, competencies outweighed race and gender in a senior executive appointment in China. Overall, Koreans had a more positive attitude toward foreign‐born Koreans than the Chinese toward foreign‐born Chinese for senior executive appointments. Implications for international human resource management and diversity management, both theoretical and applied, are discussed. © 2008 Wiley Periodicals, Inc.

KeywordsInternational Student and Expatriate Challenges; Gender Diversity and Inequality; Higher Education Governance and Development; China; Race (biology); Multinational corporation; Affect (linguistics); Gender diversity; Sample (material); Exploratory research; Psychology; Diversity (politics); Political science; Gender studies; Sociology; Management; Economics; Chromatography; Law; Anthropology; Corporate governance; Chemistry; Communication

87. Guest editors' introduction growth and globalization: Evolution of human resource management practices in Asia

Human Resource Management 2008-02-27 Available
JournalHuman Resource Management
Publication date2008-02-27
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1002/hrm.20194
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Arthur YeungChina Europe International Business School
Malcolm WarnerUniversity of Cambridge; University of Cambridge,
Chris RowleyCity University; Cass Business School, City University; Cass Business School, City University,
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Abstract In this introductory note, we offer an overview of how human resource management in Asian countries and corporations is evolving in the face of rapid business growth and integration into the global economy and we describe how the articles in this Special Issue contribute to new knowledge and insights regarding key issues, challenges, and evolution in the field of HRM in Asia. Driven by the combined forces of rapid gross domestic product growth in many Asian economies and their further integration into the global business arena, firms in Asia are in constant flux, no matter whether they are developed economies like Japan, Korea, andTaiwan; developing economies like Malaysia andThailand; or transitional economies like China and Vietnam. How will HRM systems in these countries evolve and transform under the combined forces of growth and globalization? We argue that HRM systems in these Asian firms most likely will evolve toward “bounded convergence.” The demands and expectations of the HR function to take on strategic roles (versus administrative roles) and address critical HR issues like attracting and retaining key talent, building talent pipelines, and creating high‐performing cultures are greater than ever. We conclude with a high‐level summary of the key contribution of the eight articles covered in this Special Issue. © 2008 Wiley Periodicals, Inc.

KeywordsHuman Resource and Talent Management; International Business and FDI; International Student and Expatriate Challenges; Globalization; China; Convergence (economics); Emerging markets; Face (sociological concept); Human resource management; Product (mathematics); Business; Human resources; Function (biology); East Asia; Global strategy; Economic system; Economics; Political science; Economic growth; Marketing; Management; Market economy; Sociology; Law; Finance; Mathematics; Biology; Social science; Evolutionary biology; Geometry
